title: Intel Solves 18A Yield Issues, Production Reaches 30,000 Wafers Per Month
gist: Per BlueFin Research Partners, Intel has resolved its 18A yield issues, making high-volume production economically sustainable at ~30,000 wafers/month across Arizona and Oregon fabs; Intel is now sequencing 18A-P, 18A-PT and 14A (risk 2028, HVM 2029) as its external-foundry offerings.

insights.claim: Sell-side firm BlueFin Research Partners reports Intel has fixed all 18A yield issues, making high-volume production sustainable from both a manufacturing-yield and economic standpoint.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: A key de-risking milestone for Intel Foundry's turnaround thesis; validates the node ahead of external-customer ramp.
insights.quote: Intel has fixed these yield issues, making the high-volume production of its latest node sustainable from both a manufacturing yield and economic standpoint.
insights.locator: Intel has reportedly resolved all yield issues

insights.claim: Intel's 18A yields were improving about 7% per month over several months into the launch of first 18A product Panther Lake, and the node has likely now reached mature defect rates near D0=0.1.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Quantifies the yield learning curve; D0~0.1 signals a genuinely mature node capable of profitable volume.
insights.quote: Intel confirmed that 18A yields were improving by about 7% per month over several months
insights.locator: Readers may recall that late last year

insights.claim: Intel produces 18A at Fab 52 in Phoenix, Arizona and a Hillsboro, Oregon site, together running about 30,000 wafers per month—sufficient for internal Panther Lake output but requiring more capacity for other internal products.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: 30k wpm barely covers internal needs, implying capex/expansion is still required before meaningful external foundry revenue.
insights.quote: These fabs currently produce about 30,000 wafers per month with the 18A printing.

themes.theme: Node roadmap and external foundry strategy

insights.claim: Intel has started 18A-P risk production at the D1X site in Oregon and plans to transfer the node to Fab 62 for long-term high-volume manufacturing.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Node-to-fab sequencing shows Intel executing a phased capacity plan; Fab 62 ramp is a milestone to track.

insights.claim: Early 14A results from sample production show a promising path to success, with Intel using D1X for initial high-volume production and Ohio sites for a second 14A HVM location.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: 14A is the node Intel is pitching hardest to external customers; early sample promise supports the foundry roadmap.

insights.claim: Intel's external-customer foundry offerings will focus mainly on 18A-P, 18A-PT and 14A, with 14A entering risk production in 2028 and high-volume production in 2029.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Sets the timeline for when Intel Foundry could win external design wins at scale—HVM 14A in 2029 is the key date.
